figures.caption: Table 2. For each texture type (AO, etc.) the value is the average PSNR over the dateset. PSNR and SSIM columns are the average over all textures of all texture sets of the dataset. NTCN is the method from [36] without quantization. LPE is the method from [10]. The three first line are SOTA methods. Then, the PEPS method is the middle are wrapped around the BI grid or using the same multi-resolution grid architecture as [36]. Finally, at the bottom, all those methods have have 25% fewer parameters that the rest of the table that have the same overall number of parameters.

Fig. 1. Grid-PEPS method applied to neural texture compression of a texture set of size k. (a) Given a coordinate point x ∈ R2, the method extracts Sϕ = sinxϕ and Cϕ = cosxϕ for all the targeted frequencies along the frequency curves. (b) Then for each point in P = (x, Sϕ1 , . . . , Cϕk ) it samples the grid to get latent values. (c) The result is the concatenation of the latent vector of each point. (d) The resulting vector is fed to the neural network. (e) The output of the neural network T (x) ∈ R3k is the values of the textures of the set at coordinate x. The pseudo-code is given by Algorithm 1 without the pink part.

Fig. 4. Pink aggregation applied to a latent dimension (d) of 8, and using the Fourier encoding (ϕi = 2iπ). The simple concatenation would directly concatenate the 8*7=56 values (i.e., the blue, purple, orange, and gray ones), while the Pink-PEPS only considers 8+2*4+2*2+2*1=22 values that are inversely spread given their frequency (i.e., the blue, purple, and orange ones).
Table 5. Impact of the loss, architecture, and learning rate for the Kodak dataset.
Fig. 3. (left) Power spectra of 10 textures. (right) Power spectra of the Kodak dataset. As we can see, both are following a 1 fα law [37].
